chapters.title: It's essential to know what problems you should fix first before making concrete plans for the future
chapters.text:
If you're like most business entrepreneurs, your average day is likely spent tackling problems and dealing with customer service issues. Sometimes, no matter how hard you try, your company cannot get out of crisis mode. What you require is a tool that will put an end to your firefighting efforts and set you on the path of long-term sustainability. That tool is what you will find in this summary.

Once you understand how to determine which business issue to address first, you will understand what influences your company’s growth. You'll also be able to combat the many issues that arise in workplaces and enjoy success as your company grows.

The easiest way for any entrepreneur to overcome problems is to break them down and tackle each one without pressure.

This summary gives you an insight into the business models that some of the most successful companies use. The most inspiring aspect of what you’ll find in this tidbit is that little actions and steps can push your business to greater heights.

There are five aspects of any business that most people fail to pay attention to: Sales, Profit, Order, Impact, and Legacy. These aspects of business are also known as the Business Hierarchy of Needs (BHN). The BHN is the foundation upon which a successful firm may be constructed. This topic is often discussed in business publications such as Small Business Trends, Forbes, and Investor's Business Daily.

chapters.title: To handle high-level demands appropriately, you must first deal with lower-level issues
chapters.text:
Mike Michalowicz needed more money to bring his firm out of debt; his sales soared but not his profits. He was constantly worried because he was $365,000 deep in personal debt and had a broken printer. Frustrated, Michalowicz tried to fix it by yanking and slamming his printer trays harder, hoping it would work, but it didn’t. After his sixth attempt, he realized that his tactic of slamming the printer doors didn't work.

Michalowicz leaned against the printer and discovered a paper trapped in the feed. He fixed the problem, and the machine soon began functioning again. The issue with the printer forced Michalowicz to rethink his business strategy. Increasing sales would help him get out of debt, he reasoned. Sales growth wasn't the issue since it didn't reduce debt. His main problem was that he didn't make enough profits. Since he decided to aim for profitability rather than sales, Michalowicz had to start lowering debt.

Good sales procedures are key to creating a thriving firm that can operate at maximum capacity even in your absence.

Many business entrepreneurs fail despite having plenty of money, resources, and skills. They are mostly unaware of their primary business concerns, and they suffer because they do not plan and prioritize decisions based on corporate needs. This kind of conduct is instinctual, but it seldom promotes a firm. To avoid this, we need to familiarize ourselves with author Mike Michalowicz's Business Hierarchy of Needs (BHN).

The Business Hierarchy of Needs comes in five steps in ascending order (sales, profit, order, impact, legacy). Sales are at the bottom of the hierarchy. A company or a non-profit organization needs funds constantly, so Profit stands above Sales because it provides stability and scale. Order is the next pyramid tier because an effective business method empowers personnel. Impact revolutionizes customer interactions, and Legacy ensures that a company remains after the manager retires.

chapters.title: To establish sustainability, a company must prioritize having revenue and consistent profitability
chapters.text:
When in crisis mode, you feel driven to do everything at once, from increasing sales and staffing to eliminating competitors, but this won't guarantee success. You'll ultimately accrue more debt if your finances deteriorate. Rather than attempting to fix everything at once, identify your company's weaknesses.

Companies must invest heavily in customer satisfaction through excellent services and rewards to make good sales and generate cash flow.

Imagine playing tug-of-war with a chain instead of a rope, and the chain link breaks mid-tug. It rarely matters whether you're stronger or if you’ve won; the chain is broken, and the game is over. Your company is that chain, and the best way to survive is to locate and strengthen its weakest link, known as the Vital Need.

Your Vital Need is the most significant liability in your company pyramid and must be satisfied to sustain the requirements above it. You can identify it in 15 minutes utilizing the Fix This Next four-step technique: 1. Analyze your company's demands in reference to the hierarchy of needs and identify any gaps. 2. Assess which unaddressed demand is nearest to the pyramid's base. Perhaps you aren't selling enough to pay off your obligations, which stops you from earning a profit and suggests your Sales sector isn't strong enough to sustain profit above it. 3. Set a quantifiable objective and work towards it until the need is satisfied. You can establish a monthly goal of turning a few prospective customers into sales to make money. 4. Repeat the procedure of finding the next weak connection. Using this strategy consistently will reinforce all the relationships in your company, assuring long-term viability.

The mere achievement of monthly sales targets does not prove consistency; it's more complex. Your company should not make bogus promises to clients as this leads to mistakes.

Your customers must also maintain their promises to build a strong sales foundation. Since unpaid sales aren't considered complete, declare your payment terms clearly and have a backup plan in place.

Finally, a business must be profitable to be sustainable; a corporation needs to satisfy basic needs to remain in business.

chapters.title: No matter how much goods an organization sells, it’ll be a struggle to pay bills or save cash without steady profits
chapters.text:
Many organizations have a negative money habit, especially when they squander every penny that comes their way. The more they earn, the bigger their expenses will be, which does not help in any way.

Usually, entrepreneurs reinvest profit into the business because more money implies greater products and thus more sales. But more sales don't always mean more profit, especially if delivery costs rise. Your gains must trump reinvestment if your company is to be profitable.

Most entrepreneurs see profit as surplus money that they can spend on anything the company needs, but this is wrong.

Profitability involves generating financial viability, which is essential for expanding your business without debt.

With extra cash in hand, it's easier to handle many unforeseen developments. As a result, you and your staff can move out of survival mode. To achieve long-term profitability, you must cease viewing profit as money left after meeting other financial needs. Instead, set aside a modest percentage of profit; this should offset debts and build financial reserves.

It's tempting to invest everything into growing your firm. But this won't pay off. Working towards sustainability is more beneficial than profitability.

So the only way to be sure that your solution worked is to measure it. ~ Mike Michalowicz

Mike Michalowicz

The journey to sustainability is not an easy one because corporate crises are inevitable. Some of your workers might fall sick or resign, and you might struggle to find a suitable replacement. You must pay attention to Order to avoid this since it involves working on existing business processes. It would be great if your company could run without your constant supervision.

The best way to establish Order is to create business processes that increase efficiency and prevent obstacles. You probably already have several systems that consist of work patterns. For example, your accounts team probably follows a set procedure to send an invoice. If more employees know this, your company won't lose money when someone from the accounts team is absent. That's why it's critical to identify and document work procedures. After that, store the data where reachable. If someone is missing, another person can complete the work using their processes.

chapters.title: Your company can make more impact by transitioning from being transactional to user-friendly
chapters.text:
The Lost Kitchen, owned by Erin French, uses a unique booking system. Prospective customers must mail her a postcard within the first ten days in April if they want to enjoy her private farm-to-table experience. Then they wait and hope to be one of the fortunate ones invited in the coming year.

After the restaurant garnered international recognition, French implemented a postcard system to manage reservations. Her workers were initially swamped with booking requests because there was only one dinner service every night and just a few seats available. However, she saw this inconvenience as a chance to personalize the meal experience. She came up with a plan. After the April deadline, she randomly selects postcards and assigns the bookings. Employees display the chosen postcards each night for the public to see. This small gesture established a bond between The Lost Kitchen and its guests.

When you pay attention to customer satisfaction, you create the right platform on which your business can outlast any competition.

The fourth level of the pyramid, Impact, is about producing change — for your staff and customers.

When the strength of your organization's values inspires your employees, you've made an impact, and they become brand advocates, reflecting your company's ideals. But your company's objective must be evident to accomplish this and must connect to a value workers and customers can relate to.

It's also crucial to consider your clients' experiences when interacting with your organization. Michalowicz was reminded of this when he requested chai tea from Boonton Coffee Co. The polite salesperson informed him that they only served coffee and suggested he go to a local competitor. He even gave Michalowicz instructions before encouraging him to return to the café with his tea. Michalowicz was so astounded that he altered his order to a coffee, which he received for free.

This type of experience has a significant influence. Boonton wasn't simply selling coffee; it also offered a welcoming environment for its consumers. Interactions like these always keep people coming back and make customers and strangers become brand ambassadors.

chapters.title: Your business will lose relevance if you fail to create a lasting legacy
chapters.text:
Fans of Brooklyn Nine-Nine were devastated when Fox Broadcasting Company canceled the show in May 2018. They petitioned Fox, requesting that they overturn their decision. The following day, NBC's TV network took up the show, and it was saved from extinction by its fan base.

At this point in the BHN, a brand comes alive on its own. With Brooklyn Nine-Nine, fans cared so much about the show's future that they were willing to fight to keep it on the air.

Legacy may be attained by any company, thanks to Impact. Your organization is no longer about you or your products once you've put your core principles at its heart during the Impact phase; having a positive impact on the community is what matters.

Achieving legacy entails putting processes in place to adapt to future shifts in circumstances.

If you want your firm to last beyond your lifetime, you'll have to keep up with the rapid changes in technology and society. When it comes to this evolution, Netflix is a fantastic role model. A company that once sent movies to your house via postal mail is now doing so via the Internet.

As a business owner, it is critical to assess how your company's activities align with your guiding principles every quarter. A slight detour is to be expected from time to time, and you can avoid going too far off course by making it a habit to check your current position. Establish a system that allows your company's top executives to serve as brand ambassadors so you can be confident that they'll continue to live out your vision if you remain on board after you've retired.

Did you know? According to 2020 research by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, only 25% of new businesses survive beyond 15 years.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Businesses, like humans, have a Hierarchy of Needs that they must adhere to. Although this hierarchy is important, entrepreneurs who want to drive their businesses forward rarely use it. Instead, they are frequently consumed with attempting to tackle every problem simultaneously, assigning equal importance to each. However, this does not imply progress because the most pressing issue may remain ignored, making their enterprises continue to lose relevance and sustainability.

Entrepreneurs can successfully address their market needs in order of significance by recognizing which issues they should handle first and which they should defer. This makes it easy for them to plan for potential long-term productivity and expansion.

Among all the apparent issues your business has, one and only one of them will be the most effective one at any specific moment. ~ Mike Michalowicz

Mike Michalowicz

When developing a goal as part of a solution to a business problem, that goal must be quantifiable; otherwise, you will be unable to determine whether you have achieved your objective. Begin by establishing a goal — the outcomes you hope to attain in the end. After that, figure out how you'll track your progress toward achieving your goal. Then you may evaluate your results by deciding how frequently you'll need to examine your metrics. Finally, nourish your objective by determining how often you'll alter your goals and metrics to achieve your aim.

Try this

Make a list of the challenges that your company may be experiencing and prioritize them accordingly. Make it a habit to tackle each issue one at a time and avoid giving yourself unnecessary deadlines. You can also set aside and save a part of your monthly company profits for future unforeseen circumstances.

Read it till the end?
